text: The surgeon identifies the external iliac vessel within the pelvic cavity, noting its lack of branches except for the inferior epigastric artery related to the anterior abdominal wall. The lymphadenectomy is performed parallel to the external iliac vessel, ensuring careful navigation around this anatomical structure.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy, focusing on opening the barorectal space to access the parapsychological space. The ureter is identified in relation to the uterine artery, with the deep uterine veins visible above the artery. The surgeon monitors for potential bleeding sources, noting the spatial relationship between the ureter, uterine artery, and deep uterine veins.

The surgeon performs pelvic lymphadenectomy, carefully identifying the ureter and its mesentery to avoid postoperative hydroureter. Lymphatic tissue is dissected along the veins and ligaments, including the cervix, iliopterator nodes, uterus, paravortic nodes, ovaries, vagina, and lower third of the inguinal nodes.

The surgeon identifies the boundaries of the internal iliac artery medially and the genitofemoral nerve laterally, positioned over the pelvic muscles. The dissection proceeds towards the bladder, focusing on the trigonal area near the interureteric orifices on both sides, avoiding dissection below this region.

The surgeon approaches the fascia of the rectum, carefully avoiding the rectal fat while maintaining proximity to the back of the uterus. The colpotomy is performed using a monopolar hook, with attention to the ureteric anatomy, particularly the ureteric tunnel, which is a condensation of the pelvic fascia and a vascular strand of endo-pelvic fascia.

The surgeon performs a quick closure of the vaginal cuff using hexacorporeal sutures, ensuring anatomical considerations are met, including the position of the ureter starting from the promontory and entering the pelvis medially.
The final appearance of the pelvic lymphadenectomy is shown following the completion of the radical hysterectomy. The surgical field is clear, and specimens are prepared for histological examination.
